_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Currituck County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history. Maritime county with unique record sources.
display_name: Currituck County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Currituck, NC — Courthouse Road. Located on the mainland, not the Outer Banks.

previous_courthouses.location: Currituck area — one of the original Albemarle precincts. Early courts met at various locations.

record_losses.description: Currituck County's records have survived intact. The county's remote location on the Virginia border — accessible only by water for most of its history — kept it beyond the reach of Civil War destruction. The courthouse at Currituck village has housed records since 1842.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Currituck County's records are intact, but the county's small population and maritime economy mean that many families operated largely outside the documentary record. Fishermen, watermen, and hunting guides left fewer paper traces than farmers.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Good — early colonial deeds survive from the precinct era
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Some early volumes show wear.
deeds.repository: Currituck County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from the colonial period
wills_estates.repository: Currituck County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.repository: Currituck County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes from the colonial period
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Currituck County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Very spotty until approximately 1920. Outer Banks families were particularly likely to go unregistered.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Currituck County enumerated from the first federal census. The small population makes family tracking feasible across decades.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. The maritime families of Currituck are often better documented in census records than in land records, since many did not own land or owned only the house lot.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Currituck County, NC.

substitute_sources.source_type: U.S. Life-Saving Service / Coast Guard records
substitute_sources.description: Life-saving stations along the Currituck Outer Banks employed local men as surfmen and keepers. Station logs document crews, rescues, and daily activities. These are some of the most detailed employment records available for Outer Banks families.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (Record Group 26); Coast Guard Historian's Office
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Station logs name crew members and detail daily operations. For Outer Banks families, these records provide employment documentation that is otherwise extremely rare. Surfmen were local men — the records identify families deeply rooted in the Banks community.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives, RG 26. Records of the U.S. Coast Guard.

substitute_sources.source_type: Currituck Shooting Club and hunting club records
substitute_sources.description: The Currituck Shooting Club and other waterfowl hunting clubs employed local men as guides, boatmen, and caretakers from the 1850s through the 20th century. Club records, meticulously kept by wealthy Northern sportsmen, document local employees in unusual detail.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives (Currituck Shooting Club Records); private collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Club records name local guides and caretakers, recording dates of employment, tasks performed, and sometimes personal details. For {ancestor_name}, a hunting club payroll may be the most detailed employment record in existence.
substitute_sources.source: Currituck Shooting Club Records. North Carolina State Archives, Raleigh.

narrative_hooks: Currituck County's records run back to the 1690s and have never been lost to fire or war — but for Outer Banks families who fished and guided rather than farmed, the records at the courthouse may be thin. The Life-Saving Service station logs and hunting club payrolls are the likeliest substitutes.

If {ancestor_name} was a waterfowl guide on Currituck Sound, the shooting club records may hold more detail about {pronoun_possessive} daily life than any county document — wealthy sportsmen kept meticulous records of their hunting trips, including the names of the local men who guided them.

Currituck County's vital records before 1913 are among the spottiest in NC — Outer Banks births and deaths went unrecorded more often than not. Census records and church entries are the best substitutes.

If {ancestor_name} served as a surfman at one of the Currituck life-saving stations, the station log at the National Archives documents {pronoun_possessive} employment in day-by-day detail that no other source can match.
Currituck County borders Virginia, and families crossed the state line freely — check Princess Anne County and Norfolk records if {ancestor_name} disappears from NC records.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Currituck Shooting Club Records. North Carolina State Archives, Raleigh.
National Archives, RG 26. Records of the U.S. Coast Guard (Life-Saving Service station logs).
Stick, David. The Outer Banks of North Carolina, 1584-1958. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1958.
National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Currituck County, NC.
